introduction: Follow these steps to perform the experiment on Aggregate Functions and Grouping Operations.

steps.title: Create a Database Table
steps.content: Create a table named employees with the following structure:

steps.title: Insert Sample Data
steps.content:
Insert at least 10-15 sample records into the employees table with different departments 
        (e.g., IT, HR, Finance, Sales) and varying salary values.

steps.title: Display All Records
steps.content: Use the SELECT statement to display all records from the employees table:

steps.title: Apply COUNT() Function
steps.content:
Count the total number of employees:
        
        Count employees in each department:
        SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department;
steps.code: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_employees FROM employees;

steps.title: Apply SUM() Function
steps.content:
Calculate the total salary expenditure:
        
        Calculate total salary per department:
        SELECT department, SUM(salary) AS dept_total 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department;
steps.code: SELECT SUM(salary) AS total_salary FROM employees;

steps.title: Apply AVG() Function
steps.content:
Find the average salary:
        
        Find average salary per department:
        SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department;
steps.code: SELECT AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees;

steps.title: Apply MIN() and MAX() Functions
steps.content:
Find the minimum and maximum salary:
        
        Find min and max salary per department:
        SELECT department, MIN(salary) AS min_sal, MAX(salary) AS max_sal 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department;
steps.code:
SELECT MIN(salary) AS min_salary, MAX(salary) AS max_salary 
FROM employees;

steps.title: Use GROUP BY with Multiple Columns
steps.content: Group by department and find statistics:
steps.code:
SELECT department, 
       COUNT(*) AS emp_count,
       AVG(salary) AS avg_salary,
       SUM(salary) AS total_salary
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department;

steps.title: Apply HAVING Clause
steps.content:
Find departments with more than 3 employees:
        
        Find departments with average salary greater than 50000:
        SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department 
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;
steps.code:
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department 
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3;

steps.title: Combine WHERE and HAVING
steps.content: Filter employees by age first, then group and filter groups:
steps.code:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary 
FROM employees 
WHERE age > 25 
GROUP BY department 
HAVING AVG(salary) > 40000;

steps.title: Use ORDER BY with GROUP BY
steps.code:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department 
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

steps.title: Observe and Analyze Results
steps.content:
Compare the results of different queries and understand how aggregate functions 
        work with grouping operations. Note the difference between WHERE and HAVING clauses.

expectedOutcomes: Understanding of how aggregate functions summarize data
Knowledge of GROUP BY clause for grouping rows
Understanding the difference between WHERE and HAVING clauses
Ability to write complex queries combining aggregate functions, GROUP BY, HAVING, and ORDER BY
